nodes.title: PRELIMINARY
nodes.summary: This section provides the short title, application, and commencement of the Act.

nodes.title: Definitions
nodes.summary: This section defines key terms used in the Act, such as 'Court', 'evidence', and 'fact'.

nodes.title: Evidence may be given of facts in issue and relevant facts
nodes.summary: This section outlines the admissibility of evidence regarding facts in issue and relevant facts.

nodes.title: Relevancy of facts forming part of same transaction
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of facts that are part of the same transaction.

nodes.title: Facts which are occasion, cause or effect of facts in issue or relevant facts
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of facts that are the occasion, cause, or effect of facts in issue.

nodes.title: Motive, preparation and previous or subsequent conduct
nodes.summary: This section addresses the relevance of motive, preparation, and conduct related to facts in issue.

nodes.title: Facts necessary to explain or introduce fact in issue or relevant fact
nodes.summary: This section discusses facts necessary to explain or introduce facts in issue or relevant facts.

nodes.title: Things said or done by conspirator in reference to common design
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of statements or actions by conspirators related to a common design.

nodes.title: When facts not otherwise relevant become relevant
nodes.summary: This section explains when facts not otherwise relevant may become relevant.

nodes.title: Facts tending to enable Court to determine amount are relevant in suits for damages
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of facts that help determine damages in suits.

nodes.title: Facts relevant when right or custom is in question
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of facts when a right or custom is in question.

nodes.title: Facts showing existence of state of mind, or of body or bodily feeling
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of facts showing the existence of a state of mind or body.

nodes.title: Facts bearing on question whether act was accidental or intentional
nodes.summary: This section addresses the relevance of facts in determining whether an act was accidental or intentional.

nodes.title: Existence of course of business when relevant
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of the existence of a course of business.

nodes.title: Admission defined
nodes.summary: This section defines what constitutes an admission.

nodes.title: Admission by party to proceeding or his agent
nodes.summary: This section covers admissions made by a party to the proceeding or their agent.

nodes.title: Admissions by persons whose position must be proved as against party to suit
nodes.summary: This section discusses admissions by persons whose position must be proved against a party to the suit.

nodes.title: Admissions by persons expressly referred to by party to suit
nodes.summary: This section covers admissions by persons referred to by a party to the suit.

nodes.title: Proof of admissions against persons making them, and by or on their behalf
nodes.summary: This section discusses the proof of admissions against the persons making them and by or on their behalf.

nodes.title: When oral admissions as to contents of documents are relevant
nodes.summary: This section explains when oral admissions regarding the contents of documents are relevant.

nodes.title: Admissions in civil cases when relevant
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of admissions in civil cases.

nodes.title: Confession caused by inducement, threat, coercion or promise, when irrelevant in criminal proceeding
nodes.summary: This section discusses when a confession caused by inducement, threat, coercion, or promise is irrelevant in criminal proceedings.

nodes.title: Confession to police officer
nodes.summary: This section states that confessions made to a police officer are not admissible against the accused.

nodes.title: Consideration of proved confession affecting person making it and others jointly under trial for same offence
nodes.summary: This section covers the consideration of a confession affecting the person making it and others jointly tried for the same offence.

nodes.title: Admissions not conclusive proof, but may estop
nodes.summary: This section explains that admissions are not conclusive proof but may operate as estoppels.

nodes.title: Cases in which statement of relevant fact by person who is dead or cannot be found, etc., is relevant
nodes.summary: This section outlines when statements by persons who are dead or cannot be found are relevant.

nodes.title: Relevancy of certain evidence for proving, in subsequent proceeding, truth of facts therein stated
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevancy of certain evidence for proving the truth of facts in subsequent proceedings.

nodes.title: Entries in books of account when relevant
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of entries in books of account.

nodes.title: Relevancy of entry in public record or an electronic record made in performance of duty
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of entries in public records or electronic records made in the performance of duty.

nodes.title: Relevancy of statements in maps, charts and plans
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of statements in maps, charts, and plans.

nodes.title: Relevancy of statement as to fact of public nature contained in certain Acts or notifications
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of statements as to facts of public nature contained in certain Acts or notifications.

nodes.title: Relevancy of statements as to any law contained in law books including electronic or digital form
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of statements as to any law contained in law books, including electronic or digital form.

nodes.title: What evidence to be given when statement forms part of a conversation, document, electronic record, book or series of letters or papers
nodes.summary: This section explains what evidence should be given when a statement forms part of a conversation, document, or series of letters.

nodes.title: Previous judgments relevant to bar a second suit or trial
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance of previous judgments in barring a second suit or trial.

nodes.title: Relevancy of certain judgments in probate, etc., jurisdiction
nodes.summary: This section covers the relevance of certain judgments in probate, matrimonial, admiralty, or insolvency jurisdiction.

nodes.title: Relevancy and effect of judgments, orders or decrees, other than those mentioned in section 35
nodes.summary: This section discusses the relevance and effect of judgments, orders, or decrees not mentioned in section 35.
